data.title: Older Americans, on average, watched more Winter Olympics on Peacock
data.description:
According to a report by data analytics provider Apptopia, older Americans have spent 90 minutes on average watching the 2026 Milan-Cortina Winter Olympics on their smartphones.

In the first 10 days of streaming the Winter Olympics on Peacock (CMCSA), viewership on mobile compared to previous Olympic events was lower, but the watch time of the audience was longer, the report said.

"Comparing the past three Olympic Games on Peacock, Average Time Spent per DAU is up for all age groups for these Winter Games, with the exception of the 36-45 bracket," Apptopia said. "Older users, those aged 46+, saw the largest increase over the Summer 2024 games, at 48.5%. They are watching more than 90 minutes of Olympic coverage a day on their phones."

When compared to the Summer Olympics, daily active users for the Winter event are down 9%.

However, the surge in DAUs from Day 1 to Day 10 was a whopping 92% for the Winter Olympics this year, which is much higher than the 35% rise for Winter 2022 and the 27% jump for Summer 2024 for the same duration.

Apptopia found that about 27% of users who watched the Winter Olympics on Peacock were new and had never had a session on the app before, and about 23% were "resurrected users," or those who had "churned" but re-engaged for the event. The remaining 50% were already active users on the streaming platform.

"This means the Winter Games functioned less as a pure acquisition play and more as a full-funnel lifecycle tool, reactivating lapsed subscribers while deepening engagement among the existing base," said Apptopia Director Adam Blacker.

More on Comcast

data.title: Eni weighs return to oil and gas trading, seeking rivals' big profits - FT
data.description:
Eni (E) is considering a return to oil and gas trading, as it seeks the billions in returns enjoyed by European rivals BP, Shell and TotalEnergies, the Financial Times reported Wednesday.

"I stopped trading in 2019 but the other big companies are all traders," Eni (E) CEO Claudio Descalzi told FT. "BP, Shell, Total are big traders and they make billions from that."

Descalzi also said Eni (E) is in preliminary talks with a number of commodity trading houses, including Mercuria, about forming a joint venture.

"It is not in our DNA. We are not very commercial," the CEO said. "So I thought to become commercial, we have to have a partnership to understand the business... If we can offer physical hedging, that is a big advantage for [a partner]. We can complement each other," he said, adding the amount of oil and gas that Eni (E) produces should make it an attractive partner.

Despite oil trading's profits for the major European firms, BP and Shell both noted weaker performances in their trading units over the final three months of last year.

data.title: ‘Don’t abandon U.S. markets, but have exposure in Europe’ – strategist
data.description:
Anastasia Amoroso, chief investment strategist at Partners Group, is urging investors to maintain their U.S. equity positions (SP500), (COMP:IND), (DJI) while building exposure to European markets (IEV), (IEUR), (SPEU), citing unprecedented steps by European leaders to boost economic resilience.

In an interview with CNBC, Amoroso pointed to Europe’s commitment to defense spending, new trade partnerships, and regional economic strength as reasons the continent deserves more than a passing glance from investors.

While acknowledging that U.S. markets have been “churning” compared to the rally in international stocks, Amoroso said the domestic picture remains solid. “The fundamentals of the U.S. economy are actually quite robust,” she explained, attributing recent consolidation to a “rolling AI scare” rather than any structural weakness.

The strategist highlighted several new growth engines emerging in Europe. Germany has committed to spending 5% of GDP on defense, driving industrial goods orders higher, while the European Union recently struck what Amoroso called a “monster deal” with India that cuts tariffs on 96% of European goods heading to the world’s fifth-largest economy.

Amoroso also noted a divergence between Northern and Southern Europe that provides additional stability. Countries like Spain, Portugal, and Italy have more consumer and services-oriented economies, which helps insulate them from export disruptions related to China.

When asked about concerns that regulatory burdens could stifle Europe’s growth potential, Amoroso acknowledged the issue but expressed optimism.

“The Draghi proposal is the reason why we see sort of this buildup in resilience in Europe,” she said, noting that tangible actions by European leaders are encouraging even if ultimate success remains uncertain.

The current macro environment, Amoroso argued, favors geographical diversification across multiple regions rather than concentration in any single market.

“Don’t abandon the U.S., but have exposure to Europe as well,” she advised, emphasizing that localization and supply chain shifts make broad international exposure essential for investors.

data.title: J&J to build over $1B cell therapy production site in Pennsylvania
data.description:
Johnson & Johnson (JNJ) on Wednesday announced plans to construct a cell therapy manufacturing facility in Pennsylvania at an investment of more than $1B, further boosting its business operations in the state.

The new facility to be built in Montgomery County, PA, is part of J&J’s (JNJ) efforts to spend $55B by 2029 to expand its presence in manufacturing, R&D, and technology in the U.S.

The investment is expected to generate over 500 biomanufacturing jobs and more than 4,000 construction jobs for the local economy, according to the company.

Backed by 10 facilities spanning over 2M square feet, J&J (JNJ) runs manufacturing, R&D, and office operations in Pennsylvania, adding roughly $10B to the state annually.

“For 140 years, Johnson & Johnson has been a leading innovator in American healthcare, and we are honored to continue advancing that legacy in Pennsylvania,” CEO Joaquin Duato remarked.

data.title: McDonald's attracts a bull rating from Erste Group
data.description:
Erste Group boosted its rating on McDonald's Corporation (MCD) to Buy from Hold on Wednesday.

The Austra-based research firm expects McDonald's (MCD) sales will grow more strongly again in 2026 than in the previous year. "McDonald's has a very high and stable operating margin, which is well above that of its competitors," highlighted the analyst team.

In terms of valuation, McDonald's (MCD) P/E ratio was noted to be around the sector average. However, due to the company's high profitability and good prospects, potential is seen for the multiple to stretch out and for the share price to rise further.

Wall Street analysts have a consensus Buy rating on McDonald's (MCD), while Seeking Alpha analysts are more cautious with a consensus Hold rating in place.

Shares of McDonald's (MCD) are up 7.2% on a year-to-date basis.

data.title: Quant check on Starboard Value's top Q4 holdings: QRVO, KVUE, AQN
data.description:
At the close of 2025, Starboard Value reported AUM of $5.29B, slightly lower than $5.53B a year earlier. The fund maintained largely steady assets during 2025, even as the broader market advanced 16%. The portfolio remains concentrated in technology, communication services, and healthcare sectors.

Among Starboard's Q4 top holdings is semiconductor name Qorvo (QRVO). Added in 2025 to the portfolio, the fund now holds over 7.5M shares of the company (QRVO), valued at over $634M.

Other new names added to the fund over 2025 include Bill Holdings (BILL) with over 7M shares, worth $383M; Clearwater Analytics (CWAN) worth $240M; Fluor (FLR) $205M; TripAdvisor (TRIP)  $140M; Bitcoin Infrastructure Acquisition (BIXI) worth $3M; and others.

The fund also increased its stake in KENVUE (KVUE) to over 27M shares, worth $471M, as per the fund's 13f filing. Starboard added holdings in Salesforce (CRM) by 68% to 940K shares. Rogers (ROG) shares increased to over 1.2M shares.

The stocks that Starboard Value completely exited in 2025 include Pfizer (PFE), GoDaddy (GDDY), Wix (WIX), RB Global (RBA), and others.

Companies where the fund manager reduced holdings are Match Group (MTCH), Gen Digital (GEN), Healthcare Realty Trust (HR), and News Corp (NWS).

Check SA quant ratings on Starboard Value's top 15 holdings:

  • Qorvo (QRVO) Quant rating: 3.28 – Hold
  • Kenvue (KVUE) Quant rating: 3.01 – Hold
  • Algonquin Power & Utilities (AQN) Quant rating: 2.86 – Hold
  • Bill Holdings (BILL) Quant rating: 4.57 – Strong Buy
  • Match Group (MTCH) Quant rating: 2.86 – Hold
  • iShares Core S&P Mid-Cap ETF (IJH) Quant rating: 3.56 – Buy
  • Autodesk (ADSK) Quant rating: 3.35 – Hold
  • Gen Digital (GEN) Quant rating: 3.06 – Hold
  • Salesforce (CRM) Quant rating: 3.41 – Hold
  • Clearwater Analytics (CWAN) Quant rating: 2.95 – Hold
  • Acacia Research (ACTG) Quant rating: 3.01 – Hold
  • Healthcare Realty Trust (HR) Quant rating: 3.10 – Hold
  • Fluor Corp (FLR) Quant rating: 2.94 – Hold
  • Becton, Dickinson and Company (BDX) Quant rating: 2.61 – Hold

data.title: Andersons outlines $7 EPS target for 2028 as agribusiness and renewables expansion accelerates
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: The Andersons, Inc. (ANDE) Q4 2025

Management View

  • CEO William Krueger began by thanking the team for delivering a record fourth quarter EPS, emphasizing the portfolio's versatility and resilience. He noted, "The fall harvest produced larger-than-expected volumes of grain in the Western Grain Belt and we were able to accumulate significant corn and sorghum at favorable basis values." Krueger highlighted record export programs, strong ethanol demand, and above-average yields, but acknowledged that higher corn basis and natural gas costs impacted eastern ethanol plants. He confirmed full ownership of four ethanol plants and announced an additional investment in the Clymers, Indiana facility, expected to add 30 million gallons of annual production in 2027. Krueger also detailed ongoing growth initiatives, such as upgrades to the Port of Houston, expansion of the mineral processing facility in Carlsbad, and increased processing capabilities for corn and wheat.

  • CFO Brian Valentine reported, "In the fourth quarter of 2025, the company reported net income attributable to The Andersons of $67 million or $1.97 per diluted share and adjusted net income of $70 million or $2.04 per diluted share." Valentine noted an 8% year-over-year increase in gross profit to $231 million, driven by renewables and the Skyland Grain addition. He stated, "Adjusted EBITDA for the fourth quarter was $137 million compared to $117 million in 2024 with an increase in renewables, partially offset by a year-over-year decline in the agribusiness." Valentine discussed strong cash flow from operations and highlighted that long-term debt to EBITDA at year-end was 1.8x, below the stated target of 2.5x.

Outlook

  • Krueger stated, "We expect that 2026 will bring better financial results in Agribusiness with more certainty in our global grain markets, while we believe demand for ethanol and related products will remain strong." He forecast higher-than-normal planted acres in 2026, which should benefit the fertilizer business. Krueger said, "We expect to exit 2026 with run rate EPS, more than our prior target of $4.30 and recently updated our long-range target of $7 as we exit 2028."

  • Management signaled optimism about ongoing capital projects, with expectations for completion throughout 2026 to increase efficiency and product volumes. Krueger added, "We are optimistic that year-round E15 legislation will eventually get congressional support as this would provide great benefits to the domestic ag economy."

Financial Results

  • Valentine reported a fourth quarter gross profit of $231 million and full-year gross profit of $714 million. Adjusted EBITDA for the fourth quarter was $137 million, with renewables contributing $69 million. Agribusiness reported adjusted EBITDA of $80 million for the quarter and $187 million for the year. Valentine stated, "Renewables generated fourth quarter pretax income attributable to the company of $54 million, a significant increase when compared to $17 million in 2024."

  • Cash flow from operations before working capital changes was $110 million in Q4 and $278 million for the year. The year-end cash balance declined, and short-term debt rose due to the ethanol plant acquisition. Valentine confirmed capital spending was intentionally increased for strategic projects.

Q&A

  • Benjamin Klieve, The Benchmark Company: Asked about the Skyland business performance and EBITDA contribution. Krueger responded that the strong fall harvest allowed for greater volumes than planned, while Valentine said, "It finished the year just shy of $20 million. So it was right in that range."

  • Klieve also inquired about fertilizer strategy amid uncertainty. Krueger explained, "We actually saw substantial applications of ammonia, just due to the nearly perfect application season... we feel like we'll have slightly higher than normal applications for Q1 and early Q2 in the Eastern Corn Belt."

  • Benjamin Mayhew, BMO Capital Markets: Asked about Agribusiness profit opportunities in 2026. Krueger cited more certainty around export policy, a need for increased nitrogen application, and continued biofuels policy support as profit drivers.

  • Mayhew followed up on ethanol momentum. Krueger said, "As we entered Q1, which is traditionally a lower board crush... we actually had slightly stronger board crush than I think the industry had expected." Valentine added expectations for strong export demand and $90 million to $100 million in 45Z tax credits for the year.

  • Pooran Sharma, Stephens: Asked about Skyland 2026 outlook. Krueger said, "Our expectation is probably somewhere in the $25 million to $35 million range for EBITDA." Sharma also asked about farmer selling dynamics, to which Krueger replied that higher prices would drive more selling, but timing will depend on market developments.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts maintained a positive tone, repeatedly congratulating management on strong results and focusing on growth prospects for Skyland, fertilizer, and ethanol segments.

  • Management's tone was confident and optimistic, with Krueger emphasizing resilience and stating, "We expect that 2026 will bring better financial results in Agribusiness..." Management also showed discipline in capital allocation and optimism regarding policy developments.

  • Compared to the previous quarter, the tone shifted from cautious optimism—due to challenging ag fundamentals and global trade uncertainty—to more assertive confidence in future growth and improved market conditions.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance language shifted from cautious optimism about reaching a $4.30 EPS target for 2026 to an updated long-range target of $7 by 2028. Management now expects to exceed the prior 2026 target.

  • Strategic focus advanced, with new investments in ethanol capacity, renewable feedstock infrastructure, and asset upgrades across both business segments. Analysts maintained interest in the performance of Skyland, fertilizer dynamics, and ethanol margins.

  • Key metrics improved, with record Q4 EPS, a rise in gross profit, and a substantial increase in renewables earnings compared to Q3. Management's confidence increased, especially regarding the outlook for 2026 and beyond.

  • Analysts' tone was more positive and less uncertain than in the prior quarter, reflecting improved results and clearer future guidance.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management noted external challenges, including variable harvest results, higher input costs, and the farm-gate economic environment, which could affect fertilizer volumes.

  • Krueger stated, "The current farm-gate environment is faced with challenging economics. Domestic demand for production is critical to the U.S. farmer."

  • Uncertainty in biofuels and trade policy was noted, but management indicated preparedness to act as market conditions evolve and emphasized a disciplined approach to risk management.

Final Takeaway

The Andersons delivered a record fourth quarter, driven by strong renewables and resilient agribusiness performance, and now targets a long-range EPS of $7 by 2028. The company expects improved agribusiness results and continued demand for ethanol-related products in 2026, supported by ongoing investments in growth projects and a disciplined, opportunity-driven capital allocation strategy. Management remains confident in navigating market uncertainties and is committed to optimizing shareholder value as additional acquisition and expansion opportunities arise.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Restaurant reset: Wendy's rallies amid Nelson Peltz intrigue
data.description:
The Wendy's Company (WEN) ripped a big gain in morning trading on Wednesday after Nelson Peltz disclosed in an SEC filing that it is considering what to do with its +16% stake in the restaurant company. 

Nelson Peltz believes Wendy's (WEN) stock is undervalued and is considering options such as buying more shares or selling all or part of his stake. The activist investor's relationship with Wendy's (WEN) covers roughly two decades and has included an activist campaign, control of the company through a merger, and a long tenure as its top shareholder and board leader. Peltz's Trian Fund Management began buying Wendy's (WEN) stock in 2005 and has been active in pushing for changes off and on ever since.

In 2008, after initially rebuffing him, Wendy's (WEN) agreed to be acquired in an all-stock deal by Triarc Companies, Peltz’s holding company that also owned the Arby's chain. Wendy's (WEN) strengthened its U.S. position in the 2010s and surpassed Burger King in domestic system sales, becoming the nation's fifth-largest restaurant chain by sales. However, the chain struggled in the 2020s and recently saw a same-store sales decline of 11.3% in Q4 of 2025.

Shares of Wendy's (WEN) rallied 14.8% on Wednesday and crossed back over the $8 level.

data.title: Glencore to return $2B to shareholders despite lower full-year core earnings; seals Congo land deal
data.description:
Glencore (GLCNF) (GLNCY) +3.7% in Wednesday's trading after saying it will return $2B to shareholders despite a dip in annual profit and announcing an agreement with the Democratic Republic of Congo's state mining company over a land package that allows it to boost copper production in the country.

Glencore (GLCNF) (GLNCY) reported FY 2025 EBITDA fell 6% Y/Y to $13.5B, above the $13.3B analyst consensus estimate, saying its performance reflected lower energy and steelmaking coal prices, which were partially offset by stronger metals prices, including higher zinc earnings.

Full-year adjusted EBITDA in Glencore's (GLCNF) (GLNCY) two trading units fell 8% Y/Y to $2.92B, which was attributed to a challenging energy market, as the metals trading business jumped nearly 20% while the energy and coal trading business plunged 32% Y/Y, although the latter bounced back in H2 following the unit's weakest six months on record since 2010.

The company declared total capital returns of ~$2B consisting of a $0.10/share base dividend and a $0.07/share top-up distribution of $0.07 a share.

The base dividend was broadly in line with expectations but the top-up dividend was not anticipated, Morgan Stanley analysts said, because they had forecast higher net debt than reported by the company; Glencore (GLCNF) (GLNCY) reported $11.2B net debt, compared with the $12.9B expected by the analysts.

Glencore (GLCNF) (GLNCY) said it finalized a land access agreement with Congo's state miner Gecamines for its Kamoto Copper Company operations that is expected to extend the mine's life, boost productivity, reduce costs, and secure long-term access to key ore zones previously restricted by ownership and licensing disputes.

The shareholder distribution and the Congo land deal were highlighted as positives by analysts including Ben Davis at RBC Europe, who said Glencore (GLCNF) (GLNCY) remains the top pick in his European mining coverage.

Glencore (GLCNF) (GLNCY) owns 70% of KCC, which produced ~190K metric tons of copper last year.

The Congo land access agreement after many years of delays brings the unit closer to the long-term copper output target of ~300K tons/year and mine life extension to the mid-2040s, RBC Capital analysts Ben Davis and Marina Calerothe said.

data.title: Tudor Investment adds Alphabet, exits Meta, boosts iShares Core S&P 500 ETF, among Q4 trades
data.description:
Paul Tudor Jones's Tudor Investment fund took a new stake in Alphabet, liquidated its holding in Meta Platforms (META), and increased its holdings in one S&P 500 ETF while reducing its stake in another, among its notable trades in Q4, according to its 13F filing. The fund also appeared to take some profits from a bitcoin ETF during the quarter.

New purchases included Alphabet C shares (GOOG) with ~764K shares valued at $238.9M, Sealed Air (SEE) at 4.19M shares ($173.4M), and JPMorgan Chase (JPM) at ~301K shares ($96.8M).

Exits included Spirit AeroSystems Holdings due to Boeing's (BA) purchase of the company and Meta Platforms (META), disposing of 194K shares.

The fund increased its holdings in iShares Core S&P 500 ETF (IVV) to 1.40M shares ($956.7M) from 19K shares and in Exact Sciences (EXAS) to 1.98M shares ($200.9M) from 15K shares.

It reduced stakes in State Street S&P 500 ETF (SPY) to about 445K shares ($1.47M) from 1.45M shares and in iShares Bitcoin Trust (IBIT) to ~577K shares ($28.6M) from 2.95M shares, according to the filing.
